---
title: "Introduction"
subtitle: "Project Framing and Literature Review"

format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    number-sections: true
---

## Introduction & Motivation

### Lab Task 1: The "Why"
My project focuses on ocean acidification and its impact on marine ecosystems and society. Ocean acidification is a significant environmental challenge driven by increasing atmospheric carbon dioxide (CO₂) levels, which alter seawater chemistry and threaten marine life, coastal economies, and food security. My research question is: "Why do ocean acidification levels differ among the eight major U.S. marine and Great Lakes ecosystems with different coastlines"? To investigate this question, I will compare ocean acidification data from several regions using the NOAA (National Oceanic Atmospheric Administration), National Marine Ecosystem Status database. By analyzing variations in pH and related environmental factors, I will explore the natural and human-driven causes of regional differences in acidification levels. 

This project directly connects to the Montgomery County Public Schools (MCPS) Chemistry curriculum, specifically the "Acids and Bases" unit, where students examine pH, chemical equilibrium, and the effects of changing ocean chemistry. Using authentic NOAA data will allow students to apply chemistry concepts to real-world environmental issue while strengthening their data analysis and scientific inquiry skills.The context of my project is related to "Ocean Acidification" and the impact it has in our lives. This is a pressing issue in the world we live in today. 
 
https://oceanacidification.noaa.gov/what-is-ocean-acidification/?utm_source=chatgpt.com

https://ecowatch.noaa.gov/national-status https://ecowatch.noaa.gov/thematic/ocean-acidification

https://ecowatch.noaa.gov/national-status


## Official Research Questions

Your project must be guided by clear, measurable data science inquiries. Use the format below to define your scope.

* **Primary Research Question (RQ1):** How does increasing atmospheric carbon dioxide contribute to decreasing ocean pH levels? 

* **Secondary Research Question (RQ2):**What is the relationship between ocean acidification and changes in seawater pH? Does increasing ocean acidification lead to an increase in pH level? Comparison of ocean acidification with China and India.

---

https://ecowatch.noaa.gov/thematic/ocean-acidification
## Initial Literature Review

The ocean plays a vital role in regulating Earth's climate by absorbing carbon dioxide (CO₂) from the atmosphere to maintain chemical equilibrium. As human activities have dramatically increased atmospheric CO₂ concentrations, the ocean now absorbs approximately 25–30% of these emissions, making it one of Earth's most important "carbon sinks". A carbon sink is a natural or human-made reservoir that absorbs and stores more carbon dioxide than it releases. While this process helps slow the rate of climate change, it also has unintended consequences. When CO₂ dissolves in seawater, it reacts with water to form carbonic acid, which releases hydrogen ions and lowers the ocean's pH. This process, known as ocean acidification, increases the acidity of seawater and disrupts marine ecosystems. Many organisms, including corals, oysters, clams, and some plankton, rely on carbonate ions to build shells and skeletons, and acidification reduces the availability of these ions. As a result, ocean acidification threatens marine biodiversity, fisheries, coastal economies, and communities that depend on healthy oceans. Data from the NOAA Pacific Marine Environmental Laboratory Carbon Program demonstrate a strong relationship between rising atmospheric CO₂ concentrations and increasing ocean CO₂ levels. These data also show a corresponding decline in ocean pH over time, providing clear evidence that increasing atmospheric carbon dioxide is driving ocean acidification.




Below are eight major US marine and Great Lakes ecosystem regions. Data information collected from NOAA site, National Marine Ecosystem Status 

| Major Marines and Great Lakes Ecosystem           | Source Agency / URL                    |
|---------------------------------------------------|----------------------------------------|
| Aleutian Islands pCO₂ Ecosystem Status Dataset    | National Marine Ecosystem Status       | 
| Beaufort Sea pCO₂ Ecosystem Status Dataset        | National Marine Ecosystem Status       |
| California Current pCO₂ Ecosystem Status Dataset  | National Marine Ecosystem Status       |
| Caribbean pCO2.                                   | National Marine Ecosystem Status       |                                
|  Eastern Bering Sea pCO2                          | National Marine Ecosystem Status       |
|  Gulf of Alaska pCO2                              | National Marine Ecosystem Status       |
|  Gulf of America PCO2.                            | National Marine Ecosystem Status       |
|  Northern Bering and Chukchi Seas pCO2            | National Marine Ecosystem Status       |
|  Northeast pCO2                                   | National Marine Ecosystem Status       |
|  Pacific Islands pCO2                             | National Marine Ecosystem Status       |
|  Southeast pCO2                                   | National Marine Ecosystem Status       |

::: {.callout-tip}
### Academic Scaffolding

Data showing sea surface of pCO2 and pH between the year 2019 and 2024. A significant increase in CO2 is seen in Aleutian Islands, California Current, Caribbean, Eastern Bering Sea and Gulf of America.
    
:::

| Major Marines and Great Lakes Ecosystem                                            | Sea Surface pCO2 (Between 2019 and 2024)                  | Sea Surface pH (Between 2019 and 2024)    |
|------------------------------------------------------------------------------------|-----------------------------------------------------------|-------------------------------------------|
| Aleutian Island pCO₂ Ecosystem Status Dataset (Alaska)                             |  An upward trend                                          | A small downward trend                    |
| Beaufort Sea pCO₂ Ecosystem Status Dataset (Sea of the Arctice Ocean)              | No trend                                                  | No trend                                  |
| California Current pCO₂ Ecosystem Status Dataset (Cold, slow moving ocean current) | A significant increase                                    | Significant decreasing trend              |
|  Caribbean pCO2.  (sub oceanic basin with 7,000 islands)                           | An increase in trend                                      | No significant change                     |
|  Eastern Bering Sea pCO2 (Marine cosystem off the coast of Alaska)                 | A significant increasing trend                            | significant decreasing trend              |
|  Gulf of Alaska pCO2                                                               | No significant trend                                      | No significant trend                      |
|  Gulf of America pCO2 (Oceanic basin of the Atlantic ocean)                        | A significant increasing trend                            | No significant trend                      |
|  Northern Bering and Chukchi Seas pCO2                                             | In the last 5 years, there is no apparent trend and values| In the last five years values are similar |
|  Northeast pCO2  (Refers to northeastern United States)                            | No trend                                                  | No trend                                  |
|  Pacific Islands pCO2  (Micronesia, Melanesia, and Polynesia)                      | No trend                                                  | No significant                            |
|  Southeast pCO2 (Halfway between south and east region of US)                      | No trend                                                  | No significant trend                      |

HO Find at least two academic papers, industry reports, or documentation sources relevant to your domain. Summarize their findings below and explain how your specific project builds upon or differs from their methodology.

### Prior Work 1: Data Source:

* **Source/Authors:** 

Global observations of pCO2 from automated shipboard systems that take in and analyze water while a vessel is moving are aggregated in the Surface Ocean CO2 Atlas (SOCAT; Bakker et al., 2016). These shipboard observations have been combined with data from available observational, model-based, and satellite products (e.g., sea-surface temperature, salinity, and wind speed) to create estimates of surface ocean pCO2 in U.S. Large Marine Ecosystems over multiple decades, using a technique known as machine learning (Sharp et al., 2024). The pCO2 data shown here as an ecosystem indicator of ocean acidification represent regional annual averages of these mapped estimates. Uncertainty in pCO2 is estimated from a data-based validation of the machine-learning algorithms for each Large Marine Ecosystem, scaled by the data coverage in a given month. 

* **Core Finding:** 

Description of Sea Surface pCO2:
Carbon dioxide (CO2) released from fossil fuel burning and other human activities (also known as “anthropogenic CO2”) not only accumulates in Earth’s atmosphere, but is also absorbed by seawater at the ocean surface. This process represents a double-edged sword for the planet: CO2, which has negative consequences for climate change, is removed from the atmosphere but it also contributes to the acidification of ocean waters, which can harm sea life like corals and crabs. The partial pressure of CO2 in seawater (pCO2) represents the relative amount of CO2 in the water. It is a critical metric for calculating the exchange of CO2 across the air–sea interface and for tracking the effects of anthropogenic carbon on surface ocean chemistry.

 Description of Sea Surface pH:

Man-made (or “anthropogenic”) CO2 that is taken up by the ocean influences the chemistry of seawater, resulting in an increase in hydrogen ion concentration of the surface ocean (i.e. a decrease in surface ocean pH). pH can be viewed as a “master variable” in ocean chemistry, controlling acid-base balance in the sea. It is also a common way to quantify ocean acidification: average surface ocean pH has decreased by more than 0.1 units since the beginning of the industrial revolution (Jiang et al., 2023). pH in these figures is provided on the total scale, which includes the contribution both from free hydrogen ions and hydrogen sulfate.

* **How it informs our project:** RQ1: Increasing ocean acidification leads to a decrease in ocean pH levels. As the atmosphere accumulates more carbon dioxide (CO₂), the ocean absorbs a significant portion of it. The dissolved CO₂ reacts with seawater to form carbonic acid, which releases hydrogen ions. The increase in hydrogen ions lowers the ocean's pH, making seawater more acidic.

This relationship can be summarized as follows:
1. Atmospheric CO₂ increases.
2. The ocean absorbs more CO₂.
3. CO₂ forms carbonic acid in seawater.
4. Carbonic acid releases hydrogen ions.
5. Ocean pH decreases (the water becomes more acidic).

Ocean acidification in China is driven by high anthropogenic CO₂ emissions and rapid regional sea-surface warming, particularly in the Bohai, Yellow, and East China seas. This has lowered oceanic pH and threatened commercial fisheries—such as the large yellow croaker—and coral reef ecosystems in the South China Sea.

Key Affected Regions: East China Sea (ECS) & Yellow Sea: Rapid warming along the Kuroshio current is accelerating pCO₂ absorption. The Yangtze Estuary and Hangzhou Bay face severe seasonal acidification due to both atmospheric carbon uptake and bottom-water respiration.South China Sea (SCS): Records tracking coral reef skeletons indicate anomalous and unprecedented 20th-century acidification. A significant driver of this is Pacific water injected through the Luzon Strait, which carries cumulative anthropogenic carbon into the coastal zone.
 

### Prior Work 2: 

* **Source/Authors:** Global observations of pCO2 from automated shipboard systems that take in and analyze water while a vessel is moving are aggregated in the Surface Ocean CO2 Atlas (SOCAT; Bakker et al., 2016). These shipboard observations have been combined with data from available observational, model-based, and satellite products (e.g., sea-surface temperature, salinity, and wind speed) to create estimates of surface ocean pCO2 in U.S. Large Marine Ecosystems over multiple decades, using a technique known as machine learning (Sharp et al., 2024). The pCO2 data shown here as an ecosystem indicator of ocean acidification represent regional annual averages of these mapped estimates. Uncertainty in pCO2 is estimated from a data-based validation of the machine-learning algorithms for each Large Marine Ecosystem, scaled by the data coverage in a given month. 

* **Core Finding:** 
Description of Sea Surface Ωar:

Many marine animals and plants build shells or other hard parts from the minerals aragonite and calcite, which are forms of the chemical compound calcium carbonate. The chemical building blocks of these minerals are calcium and carbonate ions, which are common constituents of seawater. However, one effect of ocean acidification is to reduce the carbonate ion concentration in seawater, making it more difficult for these minerals to form. The chemical expression that describes the tendency of these minerals to form at equilibrium is called the saturation state of that mineral: higher values support mineral formation, while lower values inhibit mineral formation, or even promote the dissolution of these minerals. We present here saturation-state estimates for the mineral aragonite (Ωar), which is used by many marine organisms including corals, mollusks, and some zooplankton to create hard parts. At 20°C, saturation state of the mineral calcite is about 50% higher than aragonite (Mucci, 1983).


* **How it informs our project:** RQ2: Increasing ocean acidification leads to a decrease in seawater pH, not an increase. Lower pH means the water is becoming more acidic. This change can reduce the availability of calcium carbonate, making it more difficult for corals, shellfish, and other marine organisms to build and maintain their shells and skeletons.

Independent variable: Ocean acidification
Dependent variable: Seawater pH
Relationship between the two variables: As ocean acidification increases, seawater pH decreases (an inverse relationship).

Ocean acidification (OA) in the Indian Ocean is driven by accelerated anthropogenic CO₂ uptake, with surface pH declining at an average rate of ~ 0.015 units per decade. This threatens India’s coral reefs, aquaculture, and the livelihoods of over 67% of fishing households. 

India faces unique, localized ocean acidification challenges determined by the contrasting behaviors of its major surrounding water bodies:The Arabian Sea: Experiences intense coastal upwelling of CO₂-rich deep waters. Combined with rising sea-surface temperatures, this accelerates local acidification and heavily impacts fragile ecosystems like the Lakshadweep and Andaman coral reefs. The Bay of Bengal: Massive freshwater influx from regional rivers lowers the water's natural buffering capacity (alkalinity). Stratification traps CO₂-rich water near the surface, while heavy aerosol pollution acts as an additional accelerator for acidification.

---

## Data Source Orientation

Global observations of pCO2 from automated shipboard systems that take in and analyze water while a vessel is moving are aggregated in the Surface Ocean CO2 Atlas (SOCAT; Bakker et al., 2016). These shipboard observations have been combined with data from available observational, model-based, and satellite products (e.g., sea-surface temperature, salinity, and wind speed) to create estimates of surface ocean pCO2 in U.S. Large Marine Ecosystems over multiple decades, using a technique known as machine learning (Sharp et al., 2024). 

Description of Sea Surface pH:

Man-made (or “anthropogenic”) CO2 that is taken up by the ocean influences the chemistry of seawater, resulting in an increase in hydrogen ion concentration of the surface ocean (i.e. a decrease in surface ocean pH). pH can be viewed as a “master variable” in ocean chemistry, controlling acid-base balance in the sea. It is also a common way to quantify ocean acidification: average surface ocean pH has decreased by more than 0.1 units since the beginning of the industrial revolution (Jiang et al., 2023). 

Data Source:

Global observations of pCO2 from automated shipboard systems that take in and analyze water while a vessel is moving are aggregated in the Surface Ocean CO2 Atlas (SOCAT; Bakker et al., 2016). These shipboard observations have been combined with data from available observational, model-based, and satellite products (e.g., sea-surface temperature, salinity, and wind speed) to create estimates of surface ocean pCO2 in U.S. Large Marine Ecosystems over multiple decades, using a technique known as machine learning (Sharp et al., 2024). The pCO2 data shown here as an ecosystem indicator of ocean acidification represent regional annual averages of these mapped estimates. Uncertainty in pCO2 is estimated from a data-based validation of the machine-learning algorithms for each Large Marine Ecosystem, scaled by the data coverage in a given month.
